# Figure description: Страт_колонки_скважин_черт_49_л_1.jpg

---

## Figure Type

This is a **composite stratigraphic correlation diagram** (литолого-стратиграфический корреляционный разрез) showing multiple borehole/well logs correlated side by side. It is a multi-column lithological section typical of Soviet-era exploration reports, where individual borehole cores are depicted as vertical columns with standardized lithological symbols, and correlation lines are drawn between equivalent stratigraphic horizons across boreholes.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The figure correlates **multiple boreholes** (скважины) drilled in the survey area of northern Kazakhstan (map sheets M-42 and/or N-42). The diagram appears to show **6–8 or more individual borehole columns** arranged across the page, connected by diagonal correlation lines linking equivalent stratigraphic units or marker horizons. The boreholes are spread across what appears to be a regional transect. Some borehole designations are partially legible and appear to include references to **скв** (скважина = borehole) with numbers, though specific numbers are largely **[illegible]** due to scan quality. Depth ranges appear to extend to several hundred metres based on the column lengths. References to **параметрический разрез** (parametric section) are visible in several column headers.

---

## Key Content

### Overall Structure
- The figure contains approximately **7–9 vertical lithological columns** of varying lengths, arranged roughly left to right across the page
- **Diagonal correlation lines** connect equivalent stratigraphic levels between adjacent and non-adjacent boreholes, indicating the interpreter's interpretation of lateral continuity of rock units
- The columns vary significantly in total length (depth penetrated), with the **central column** appearing to be the deepest and longest

### Lithological Symbols
The columns display a variety of standard Soviet lithological symbols (штриховки), including:
- **Dotted/stippled patterns** — likely sandstones or sandy sediments
- **Brick/block patterns** — likely limestones or crystalline carbonates
- **Horizontal line patterns** — likely clays, siltstones, or shales
- **Cross-hatch and irregular patterns** — likely conglomerates, breccias, or weathered crystalline basement
- **Diagonal hatching at the base of some columns** — possibly crystalline basement (metamorphic or igneous rocks)

### Stratigraphic Units
Multiple stratigraphic horizons are labeled along the columns (formation codes, age designations). Partially legible labels suggest:
- References to Paleozoic units (possibly Devonian, Carboniferous, or Permian)
- Possibly Mesozoic cover sediments in upper portions
- Crystalline basement encountered at depth in some boreholes
- Stratigraphic index labels of the form **T**, **P**, **C**, **D** or similar (Triassic, Permian, Carboniferous, Devonian) are visible at various levels

### Correlation Lines
- Lines drawn between columns show that certain key beds (marker horizons) are **laterally continuous** across the section
- Some beds thin or thicken between boreholes, suggesting structural variability
- The correlation pattern implies a broadly **layered to gently deformed stratigraphy** with possible unconformities

### Annotations
- Several columns are annotated with formation names or unit codes at the left or right margins
- Small text boxes appear near some correlation lines, possibly indicating **formation names**, **age assignments**, or **depth values** — most are **[illegible]** at this scan resolution
- A **legend box** (условные обозначения) appears in the lower right corner, containing lithological symbols and their descriptions — specific entries are **[illegible]**
- A **stamp/seal box** is visible in the lower right, consistent with Soviet archival documentation practice
- The number **59** is handwritten in the lower right area, likely a figure or sheet number

## Research Value

This figure is a **key regional stratigraphic correlation tool**, allowing a geologist to assess the lateral continuity, thickness variation, and depth of individual rock units across multiple drill sites in the survey area. It directly answers questions about **subsurface stratigraphic architecture** — which units are persistent across the region, where unconformities exist, and where basement is encountered — all of which are fundamental to evaluating **mineral and hydrocarbon potential**.
